does: This paper articulates the framework for evaluating AI systems against extreme risks by distinguishing two complementary evaluation approaches: dangerous capability evaluations that identify whether models possess potentially harmful abilities like offensive cyber skills or sophisticated manipulation, and alignment evaluations that assess the propensity of models to misuse those capabilities. The work emphasizes that as AI systems become more powerful, standard benchmarks measuring task performance are insufficient without explicit assessment of both what dangerous capabilities models possess and whether their deployment characteristics create unacceptable risks. The paper provides conceptual grounding for why such evaluations matter practically and ethically for responsible AI development.
connects: This work builds on emerging concerns within AI safety about the divergence between model capabilities and their intended uses, extending earlier research on adversarial robustness, interpretability, and alignment measurement toward the specific domain of extreme risks. It connects to prior work on capability evaluation frameworks and red-teaming methodologies while responding to the growing realization that powerful general-purpose AI systems develop capabilities orthogonal to their training objectives. The paper engages with policy-focused AI governance discussions and complements technical safety research by providing a bridge between capability measurement and deployment decisions that developers and institutions must make.
enables: This framework enables the development of concrete evaluation protocols and benchmarks specifically designed to detect high-stakes risks before model deployment. The distinction between capability and alignment evaluations sets up future work on measuring model propensity toward harmful application of abilities, including studies of how model size, training procedures, and deployment contexts affect misuse risk. It points toward institutional processes where dangerous capability evaluations inform security decisions, deployment restrictions, and further research priorities. The work also establishes foundations for policy-developer collaboration where evaluation results inform regulatory approaches, liability frameworks, and corporate governance structures for managing extreme risks in increasingly powerful AI systems.
